questions.question_text: How many members serve on the Hawaii Real Estate Commission?
questions.explanation: HIREC consists of 9 members appointed by the Governor and confirmed by the Senate. The Governor also designates the chair from among the members.

questions.question_text: A candidate for HIREC must hold a broker license with at least how many years of Hawaii real estate experience?
questions.explanation: At least 4 of HIREC's 9 members must be licensed brokers with a minimum of 3 years of Hawaii real estate experience. Two members must be public (non-licensee) members.

questions.question_text: A HIREC seat reserved for Kauai becomes vacant. Who selects the replacement?
distractors: The Senate president fills the seat
questions.explanation: All 9 HIREC members are appointed by the Governor and confirmed by the Senate. The geographic distribution requires 4 from Honolulu and 1 each from Kauai, Maui, and Hawaii County.

questions.question_text: How many public (non-licensee) members must serve on HIREC?
questions.explanation: HIREC requires at least 2 public members who are not real estate licensees. This ensures consumer representation on the commission alongside the minimum 4 licensed broker members.

questions.question_text: HIREC commissioners serve 4-year terms. How are they compensated?
distractors: They receive a per diem stipend
They are paid per meeting attended
questions.explanation: HIREC members serve uncompensated 4-year terms. They are appointed by the Governor and confirmed by the Senate but do not receive any salary or per diem for their service.

questions.question_text: Which division within DCCA houses the Hawaii Real Estate Commission?
questions.explanation: HIREC operates within the Department of Commerce and Consumer Affairs (DCCA), specifically under the Professional and Vocational Licensing (PVL) Division. The Real Estate Branch provides staff support.

questions.question_text: HIREC oversees education programs for licensees. Which fund helps support this function?
questions.explanation: HIREC administers the Real Estate Education Fund, which supports real estate education programs. HIREC also administers the Condominium Education Trust Fund, which supports condo-related education.

questions.question_text: In addition to licensing, HIREC administers the Recovery Fund and performs which other regulatory function?
questions.explanation: HIREC's powers include granting and denying licenses, adopting rules, administering the Recovery Fund, registering condominiums, and overseeing education. It also administers the Real Estate Education Fund and Condominium Education Trust Fund.

questions.question_text: A broker wants to know which Hawaii statute directly governs real estate licensing. Which chapter applies?
questions.explanation: HRS Chapter 467 is the primary statute governing real estate licensing in Hawaii. HRS Chapter 436B provides the uniform framework applicable to all professional and vocational licenses.

questions.question_text: Which entity provides day-to-day staff support for the Hawaii Real Estate Commission?
questions.explanation: The Real Estate Branch (REB) within the PVL Division of DCCA provides staff support to HIREC for its licensing, education, and regulatory functions.

questions.question_text: A consumer believes a licensee committed fraud. To file a complaint with RICO, what fee must the consumer pay?
questions.explanation: Filing a complaint with RICO (Regulated Industries Complaints Office) is free. The consumer does not need to pay any fee or hire an attorney to initiate a complaint against a licensee.

questions.question_text: RICO serves as the enforcement arm of which Hawaii department?
questions.explanation: The Regulated Industries Complaints Office (RICO) is the enforcement and investigative arm of the Department of Commerce and Consumer Affairs (DCCA). It handles complaints against licensees regulated by DCCA boards and commissions.

questions.question_text: After RICO completes a field investigation, what is the next step in the enforcement process?
questions.explanation: The RICO enforcement process follows a sequence: intake, field investigation, legal review, and then enforcement action. A hearing, if needed, follows the procedures under HRS Chapter 91 (Administrative Procedure Act).

questions.question_text: A licensee facing HIREC disciplinary action requests a formal hearing. What procedural protections apply?
distractors: No right to present evidence or witnesses
questions.explanation: Licensees facing discipline have the right to a formal administrative hearing with full due process protections under the Administrative Procedure Act, including the right to present evidence, call witnesses, and have legal representation.

questions.question_text: A consumer wants to file a RICO complaint but cannot afford an attorney. Can the complaint still be filed?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, no attorney is needed to file
distractors: Only if income is below poverty level
Only through a licensed broker advocate
questions.explanation: Consumers can file complaints with RICO without an attorney and at no cost. The process is designed to be accessible, requiring only a written complaint describing the alleged violation.
